phenomenon: variability in normative judgment across contexts
claim: Normative judgment shifts from objectivist to relativist framing as targets become culturally distant, suggesting no fixed underlying stance but context-dependent adoption of viewpoints.

phenomenon: intuition reliability in abstract judgment
claim: Ordinary people lack the context-sensitive intuitions philosophers attributed to them; apparent disagreement may be error or confusion rather than genuine context-dependent knowledge judgments.

phenomenon: lay theories of causal absence
claim: Ordinary people hold implicit metaphysical views about causation that differ from expert philosophical accounts and deserve empirical investigation.

phenomenon: interaction of emotion and deliberation in normative judgment
claim: Increased deliberation shifts judgments by allowing reasoned argument to override automatic emotional resistance, with magnitude depending on argument strength.

phenomenon: expectation-formation and life-course transition timing
claim: Early expectations about reproductive timing predict educational pathway choice, but actual reproductive events have weaker causal effects on persistence than expected.

phenomenon: cessation of intimate behavior in long-term pair bonds
claim: Cessation of intimate activity increases with time and health decline; bond quality and relationship history show gender-differentiated associations.

phenomenon: lay understanding of agency and determinism
claim: Folk intuitions about agency are compatibilist when people correctly understand determinism; apparent incompatibilism results from misinterpreting determinism as mental-bypass.

phenomenon: cognitive control in cross-language sound processing
claim: Stronger inhibitory control predicts more accurate perception and production of target-language sounds, suggesting control enhances phonological discrimination.

phenomenon: consequence-sensitivity in knowledge attribution
claim: Stakes shift knowledge attribution: identical evidence warrants less knowledge-credit when consequences of error are high.

phenomenon: inertia in preference formation
claim: Preset options shape aggregate preference through inertia; changing defaults produces large shifts in behavior.

phenomenon: metaphorical linking of bodily and moral purity
claim: Moral transgression activates cleansing desire and cleansing behavior reduces moral distress, linking bodily purity concepts to moral self-restoration.

phenomenon: collective action and cooperation in shared-resource governance
claim: Costly punishment occurs in shared-resource dilemmas but benefits depend critically on combining punishment with communication, not punishment alone.

phenomenon: cleansing and cognitive resolution of conflicting preferences
claim: Physical cleansing eliminates postdecisional preference distortion, suggesting cleansing metaphorically resets past concerns.

phenomenon: tactile properties unconsciously shaping social perception
claim: Incidental tactile properties (weight, texture, hardness) nonconsciously bias social judgment in metaphor-congruent ways.

phenomenon: automatic encoding and retention of others' mental states
claim: Others' beliefs are automatically computed and maintained even when irrelevant and conflicting, shaping perception and action without explicit awareness.

phenomenon: retrieval-based learning and memory integration
claim: Retrieval practice produces stronger memory encoding by generating mediators that are both retrievable and effective at target-elicitation.

phenomenon: habituation via mental simulation
claim: Repeated imagination of stimulus-consumption produces habituation identical to actual consumption, mediated by reduced desire not reduced palatability.

phenomenon: retrieval-driven versus elaborative learning pathways
claim: Retrieval practice produces greater conceptual learning gains than elaborative study, even on conceptual transfer tasks.

phenomenon: expressive disclosure and pressure-performance coupling
claim: Expressive writing about test concerns immediately before high-stakes performance reduces worry-induced performance decrements.

phenomenon: offloading of memory to external information systems
claim: When people expect external information access, they shift from encoding facts to encoding retrieval locations, treating external systems as distributed memory.
